Welcome to the Art of Email (AoE). We’re glad you’re here.
AoE will teach you how to create audience-centric, serialized,
story-driven emails that:
▪ Earn and sustain your audience’s attention.
▪ Deliver your marketing messages effectively.
▪ Move readers in directions that bene�t them (and you).
▪ Create, channel, and intensify desire for the value you offer.
▪ Build long-term relationships with prospects and customers.
▪ Create happy† customers, fans, and superfans over time.
Introduction
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

† For us, “happy” is a meaningful distinction. There’s a world of
di�erence between generating a customer (through any means
possible to maximize sales), and a very speci�c customer who feels an
emotional pull to want to interact with us more intimately
(through our courses, emails, workshops, and free trainings).
Happy customers don’t want refunds, often tell others how happy
they are, remain loyal for long periods of time, and are also more
inclined to purchase more (and more) over time, and in many cases,
everything on o�er.
AoE is built on the premise that your email marketing is a system
that produces emergent results, not a collection of random parts
with different jobs to accomplish.
As a result, AoE campaigns work together — systemically — to
help you reach more people, create more value, earn more money,
and feel good about the way you’re showing up in the world.
We call that marketing with integrity.
The Art of Email is a time-tested distillation of ideas we’ve
developed and re�ned over more than four decades of combined
experiences in the trenches of online marketing.
AoE rests on a foundation with four core components:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

▪ People First
▪ Principles of Systems Theory
▪ Elements of Jay Abraham’s Strategy of Preeminence
▪ Strategic Upstream Decisions
People First
Behind every number we can measure, are real people with real
problems to solve (or avoid) and meaningful desires to ful�ll. We
believe that understanding people is far more important than
interpreting click rates, open rates, and other metrics.
When we view our email marketing through the lens of people
�rst, and embrace the Golden Rule, everything changes for the
better. It’s as simple as that.
AoE will teach you how to deliver real, sustained value that helps
your audience achieve their goals, solve their problems, and ful�ll
their desires as those relate to your areas of expertise.
AoE also recognizes that your audience’s goals, problems, and
desires have functional, social, and emotional dimensions which
creates opportunities to create more engaging experiences for
your readers — pulling them forward rather than pushing them
along.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

Some of those people will want to buy from you immediately. AoE
will show you how to identify that subset of your audience and
create a path speci�cally for them.
Most of your audience who will eventually become customers —
85% by Dean Jackson’s estimate — won’t buy until after they’ve
been in your world for several months. AoE will show you how to
engage effectively with that audience as well.
Rather than a one-size-�ts-all approach, AoE will show you how to
engage with your entire audience in ways that work for everyone,
no matter where they are on their journey to become a happy
customer, fan, or superfan.
Principles of Systems Theory
Rather than dive deep into the complexities of systems theory,
we’ll focus on the few critically important elements that inform
The Art of Email. Those are:
▪ A system is a whole that contains two or more parts, each of
which can affect the properties or the behavior of the whole.
▪ None of the parts of a system have an independent effect on
the whole. How any part affects the whole depends on what
the other parts are doing.
▪ The results we can see and measure — like feedback, opens,
clicks, and purchases — are emergent properties of the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

whole system.
An easily understood example that explains the basics of systems
theory is an automobile. The essence of a functional automobile is
movement — taking its occupants from one point to another (down
the street or across the country).
To accomplish that task, automobiles have a variety of subsystems
that work together, including an engine, drivetrain, fuel delivery
system, electronics, aerodynamics, etc.
When those parts are combined in speci�c ways, movement
emerges as a result. However, none of the parts of the automobile,
on their own, are capable of movement. An engine, by itself,
doesn’t move occupants from point A to point B. Nor does any
other part of the whole automobile.
It’s the intelligently-designed relationships among the parts of
the automobile that make movement, speed, handling, and
comfort possible.
The relationships among the parts of the automobile are critically
important too. Imagine combining the engine from a Formula 1
race car with the drivetrain of a diesel truck and the fuel system of
a Fiat 500 and you’ll quickly realize how important those
relationships are!
The same systemic principles apply to email marketing (and more
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

broadly, business in general).
Individual emails and email campaigns work together to create
the results you and your audience experience. No single email,
email campaign, or marketing message is responsible for
results.
Instead, a combination of each prospect’s context (needs, desires,
awareness, etc.), the tone of your communications, the match (or
mismatch) between your offer and your audience’s needs, and
many other factors interact to produce results.
As we’ll explain in detail later in the course, The Art of Email is a
�nely-tuned system where each part works together to create a
cohesive, powerful whole that you can expand and improve —
systemically — over time.
We’ll also explain how those parts relate to and interact with each
other for maximum effect.
The Strategy of Preeminence
The Art of Email embraces a mindset informed by key principles
from Jay Abraham’s Strategy of Preeminence.
In Jay Abraham’s words:
The Strategy of Preeminence demands that from this moment forward,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

you see yourself in the relationship you have with your clients as their
most trusted advisor, counsel, con�dant, in the �eld of endeavor, in the
area of expertise, and the outcome area that your product or service
produces in their life or in their business.
You want to be seen from now on by everyone you deal with as the
de�nitive expert source, and the most trusted advisor, and as a
�duciary — as someone who has taken on the responsibility of their
governance, their decisions, their best interests, as their most trusted
counsel.
We understand that making this shift in the way you see your
audience, your business, and yourself is not something that
happens overnight. However, we can’t stress enough how
powerful this mindset shift can be.
The shift in perspective that follows adoption of the Strategy of
Preeminence is profound. You’ll begin to habitually look for ways
to create more value for your audience (which will create, in
return, more value for you).
You’ll no longer see yourself as one of many options for your ideal
clients. Instead, you’ll see yourself and the value you create as the
de�nitive best option (and, as a result, you continually re�ne your
business, your offers, and your correspondence to re�ect that
position).
Adopting the Strategy of Preeminence also changes how you
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

approach the work of writing emails. Instead of taking the
perspective that you “have to” write emails to share your value
with the world, you begin to realize that you “get to” do that.
Borrowing a favorite phrase from Mark Twain, that distinction is
the difference between the lightning bug and lightning.
Strategic Upstream Decisions
Finally, AoE is informed by four Strategic Upstream Decisions
we’ve compiled and re�ned over decades. These are our ‘rules of
the road’ adopted to eliminate problems rather than try to solve
them after the fact.
“I don’t want to be a great problem solver. I want to avoid problems
—prevent them from happening and doing it right from the
beginning.” — Peter Bevelin (Seeking Wisdom: From Darwin to
Munger)
Moving our decision-making as far upstream as possible creates
leverage. Dan Heath shares an example of why this is important
in his book Upstream: The Quest to Solve Problems Before They
Happen.
Upstream opens with a public health parable about two people
rushing to save a child who is drowning in a stream. Just as one
person pulls that child to safety, another child appears. And the
moment that second child is pulled to safety, another appears.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

This continues for a while as more children appear until the
rescuers are nearly exhausted.
“Suddenly, you see your friend wading out of the water, seeming to
leave you alone. ‘Where are you going?’ You demand. Your friend
answers, ‘I’m going upstream to tackle the guy who’s throwing all these
kids in the water.'”
Strategic decisions each represent one decision that eliminates
the need for dozens, perhaps hundreds, of others.
Business researcher and author Jim Collins credits Peter Drucker
with this idea. During a 2018 interview with Tim Ferriss (that we
highly recommend) he said:
“Don’t make a hundred decisions when one will do…Peter believed that
you tend to think that you’re making a lot of di�erent decisions. But
then, actually, if you kind of strip it away, you can begin to realize that
a whole lot of decisions that look like di�erent decisions are really part
of the same category of a decision.”
The Art of Email is informed by four Strategic Upstream
Decisions we’ve developed, tested, and re�ned over 40+ years.
Those are:
▪ Emphasize relationships before transactions.
▪ Treat everyone like a customer, regardless if money has
changed hands, because customers are created before they
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

buy anything.
▪ Build attractive worlds for your audience to inhabit.
▪ Ask for explicit permission before sharing promotions.
We’ll examine each of these decisions in the following four
entries in Module 1.
You may be tempted to skip ahead and get to the ‘good stuff.’
Don’t.
Understanding and internalizing these decisions will pay
signi�cant dividends in every aspect of your marketing and — as
we’re sure you’ve guessed already — these decisions work
together, systemically, to produce even more powerful results.
Please ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
